question.text: Rajesh is evaluating two portfolios. Portfolio A has an expected return of 12% and standard deviation of 8%. Portfolio B has an expected return of 14% and standard deviation of 12%. If the risk-free rate is 6%, which portfolio has a higher Sharpe Ratio and what is the difference between them?

answer.text: Both portfolios have the same Sharpe Ratio

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Sharpe Ratio = (Rp - Rf) / σp. Portfolio A: (12% - 6%) / 8% = 0.75. Portfolio B: (14% - 6%) / 12% = 0.6667. Difference = 0.75 - 0.6667 = 0.0833. Portfolio A delivers more return per unit of total risk. **The Trap:** Many candidates pick Portfolio B because it has a higher absolute return of 14%, ignoring that Sharpe Ratio adjusts for risk. Higher return does not mean better risk-adjusted performance. **Cross-Exam Context:** Sharpe Ratio calculations are tested in NISM Series V-A (Mutual Fund Distributors) and Series XV (Research Analyst) exams as well. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always compare portfolios on risk-adjusted returns, not absolute returns. A portfolio with lower return can still be superior if its risk is proportionally much lower.
